title: The DevOps Lifecycle
description:
## The Infinity Loop

The DevOps lifecycle is usually drawn as an **infinity (∞) loop** rather than a straight line, because software is never "done" — every release feeds learning back into the next plan. The loop is continuous and the two halves represent **Dev** (left) and **Ops** (right).

### The eight phases

```
   Plan → Code → Build → Test → Release → Deploy → Operate → Monitor
     ↑                                                          |
     └──────────────── feedback ────────────────────────────────┘
```

| Phase | What happens | Typical tooling |
|---|---|---|
| **Plan** | Define requirements, break work into small items, prioritize the backlog. | Jira, GitHub Issues, Boards |
| **Code** | Write the application and infrastructure code; review via pull requests. | Git, IDEs, code review |
| **Build** | Compile/package the code into an artifact (e.g. a container image). | Maven, npm, Docker |
| **Test** | Run automated tests — unit, integration, security, performance. | Jest, pytest, JUnit |
| **Release** | Version and stage a tested artifact, ready to go to production. | Artifact registries, release tags |
| **Deploy** | Push the release into an environment (staging, then production). | Argo CD, Helm, Terraform |
| **Operate** | Run the system: scaling, configuration, incident response. | Kubernetes, cloud platforms |
| **Monitor** | Collect metrics, logs and traces; alert on problems; learn. | Prometheus, Grafana, ELK |

Monitoring feeds insight back into **Plan** — closing the loop. A bug found in production becomes a backlog item; a slow endpoint becomes next sprint's optimization.

### CI and CD: the automation engine

The middle of the loop — **Code → Build → Test → Release → Deploy** — is where automation pays off the most. That is what people mean by a **pipeline**.

**Continuous Integration (CI)** sits around **Build + Test**. Every time a developer merges code, an automated pipeline builds it and runs the test suite. The point is to catch integration problems *early* and *often*, instead of in a painful "merge week" at the end. If the build or tests fail, the team fixes it before moving on.

**Continuous Delivery / Deployment (CD)** picks up where CI ends — at **Release + Deploy**:

- **Continuous Delivery** keeps the application **always in a releasable state**. Every change that passes CI is automatically prepared for release, but the actual push to production is a **manual, one-click decision**.
- **Continuous Deployment** goes one step further: every change that passes the pipeline is **automatically deployed to production** with no human gate.

```
CI:  merge → build → test            (automated, on every change)
CD:  → release → deploy              (Delivery = manual go; Deployment = automatic)
```

> Together, CI/CD turns the lifecycle from a series of risky, manual handoffs into a smooth, automated flow — which is exactly what shortens lead time and lowers risk.

Mark this lesson complete when the loop and the difference between CI and CD make sense to you.
